design_philosophy.core_loop: Multi-stage tactical infiltration: position snipers to cover approach → provide sniper support as rappellers descend → search embassy interior for hostages while eliminating terrorists
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing operational speed against precision—faster completion risks hostage/operative casualties, but perfection requires careful, deliberate play. Searchlights force stealth timing; sniper support creates dependency between team elements.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command a GIGN counterterrorism team executing a real-world hostage rescue operation with multiple specialized roles (snipers, rappellers, entry team) working in coordinated sequence
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Casualty-aware game design: mission success is not binary. The game explicitly grades outcomes by hostage survival, operative safety, and operational completion—different endings reward different priority balances. This is unusual for NES-era action games, suggesting a design philosophy that espouses consequence and nuance over arcade perfection.

difficulty.lives_system: Operatives can take damage and die; loss of operatives reduces support capacity in later sections
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression: failure in one section (searchlight, sniper phase, or interior) requires restart of that section; overall mission completion is cumulative
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Difficulty levels renamed to military ranks in US version (original Japanese used numbered difficulty). Player can select difficulty before attempting mission, suggesting adjustable challenge curve. Mission outcomes scale based on performance metrics (speed, accuracy, casualties).

progression.unlock_mechanism: Three distinct sequential gameplay sections unlock via narrative progression: sniper positioning → sniper support/rappel descent → interior hostage rescue. No equipment or skill upgrades between sections.
replayability_hooks: Four distinct ending states based on casualty combinations (perfect rescue, hostages lost, operatives lost, combined losses)
Difficulty rank selection (titled military ranks in US version)
Performance grading system evaluated at mission end (speed, accuracy, hostage count, operative survival)
Newspaper ending summary provides narrative consequence feedback

world_building_techniques: Real-world setting: Paris embassy hostage crisis with GIGN counterterrorism team
Role-based character names (Delta, Echo, Mike snipers; Hotel, Tango, Bravo entry team) establish operational structure
Newspaper excerpt ending summarizes operation outcome and consequences
Regional narrative variance: Japanese and US/EU versions feature different ending text emphasizing mission success criteria differently

faction_indicators: GIGN (French national counterterrorism unit) as player faction
Hostages as civilian faction requiring rescue
danger_signals: Searchlights scanning street: environmental threat indicator
Terrorist silhouettes visible in embassy windows: sniper target identification
Active gunfire during descent: real-time threat escalation
Hostage location discovery: protected entity requiring safe extraction
environmental_storytelling: Embassy building layout communicates operational geography
Sniper vantage points across street establish tactical geography
Searchlight patterns indicate terrorist defensive posture
Window breaching reveals interior threats progressively
Floor maps in some ports show entity locations, building operational complexity

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Rescue: The Embassy Mission (regional differences, ending variations)
Wikipedia - Hostages (video game) (gameplay mechanics, development, reception, platform information)
